3	\chapter{The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of \(\mathscr L(D)\)); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor \([P]\) of a closed point is the one of
94	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order \(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	REF and REF.
98	
99	\section{The line bundle of a closed point on a curve}
100	
101	% --- iter-187 plan-phase additions: pins for the iter-187 refactor's
102	% Step 3 + Step 4 sub-helpers (carrierPresheaf functor + sheaf property)
103	% which are the direct-construction substrate the definition below
104	% consumes (via the iter-186 plan-phase `analogies/ocofp-carrierset-submodule-api.md`
105	% Decision 3 + Decision 4 recipe). The Lean declarations are produced
106	% by the iter-187 refactor-`ocofp-steps3to5` dispatch. The substantive
107	% mathematical content is in the Definition block below; these helper
108	% blocks pin the substrate hooks the prover-side construction uses.
109	
110	\begin{definition}
111	\leanok
112	[Presheaf carrier for the line bundle of a closed point]
113	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf}
114	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierPresheaf}
115	  For each open \(U \subseteq C\), the \(\bar k\)-submodule
116	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U) \subset K(C)\) of section candidates
117	  satisfying the order conditions ``\(\mathrm{ord}_Q f \ge 0\) for all
118	  prime divisors \(Q \in U\) other than \(P\), and additionally
119	  \(\mathrm{ord}_P f \ge -1\) when \(P \in U\)'' assembles into a
120	  \(\bar k\)-linear functor
121	  \[
122	    \texttt{carrierPresheaf}_P : (\Opens\, |C|)^{\op}
123	    \longrightarrow \ModuleCat_{\bar k},
124	    \qquad U \longmapsto \mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U),
125	  \]
126	  with restriction \(V \subseteq U \mapsto \texttt{Submodule.inclusion}\,
127	  (\texttt{carrierSet\_mono})\) on each pair. The functor laws follow from
128	  the antitone monotonicity \(U \mapsto \mathrm{carrierSet}_P(U)\) (witness
129	  \(\texttt{carrierSet\_mono}\) at L181-192 of the Lean file) by
130	  \(\texttt{ext}\,\langle x, \_\rangle\); \texttt{rfl}.
131	\end{definition}
132	
133	\begin{lemma}
134	\leanok
135	[Sheaf property of the carrier presheaf]
136	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
137	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
138	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf,
139	        def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
140	  The presheaf \texttt{carrierPresheaf}\(_P\) (defined via the
141	  carrier-refinement of \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf})
142	  is a sheaf in the appropriate Grothendieck topology on \(C\). Concretely,
143	  the locality + gluing conditions for \(\ModuleCat_{\bar k}\)-valued sections
144	  reduce (via \(\texttt{isSheaf\_iff\_isSheaf\_forget}\)) to the sheaf
145	  condition on the underlying Set-valued functor: each
146	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(U)\) is a subset of the constant
147	  \(K(C)\)-sheaf restricted to \(U\), and the sheaf property in the
148	  constant-sheaf base case is direct. The project's
149	  \texttt{toModuleKSheaf} template
150	  (\texttt{Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/SheafProperty.lean}) is
151	  the model for this argument.
152	\end{lemma}
153	
154	\begin{definition}
155	
156	\leanok
157	[Carrier-submodule refinement enforcing \(F(\bot)=0\)]
158	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
160	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf}
161	  Iter-188 Lane A discovery: the raw \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P\) of
162	  \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf} satisfies
163	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(\bot) = K(C)\) (since the order conditions
164	  are vacuously satisfied on the empty open). This violates the sheaf
165	  axiom on the Grothendieck topology of opens: the empty sieve covers
166	  \(\bot\), forcing \(F(\bot) =\) terminal \(= 0\) on
167	  \(\ModuleCat_{\bar k}\)-valued functors.
168	  The refinement
169	  \[
170	    \mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(U) \;:=\;
171	    \mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U) \;\sqcap\; \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U,
172	    \qquad \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U \;:=\;
173	    \{\, f \;\mid\; U \ne \bot \,\lor\, f = 0 \,\}.
174	  \]
175	  forces \(F(\bot) = \bot\) (intersection annihilates) while preserving
176	  \(F(U)\) on non-empty \(U\) (since then \(\mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U = \top\)).
177	  The case-based restriction map (zero target on \(\bot\); inclusion
178	  otherwise) gives a well-formed sheaf-condition substrate consumed by
179	  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}. Downstream
180	  consumers \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}, \texttt{toFunctionField},
181	  \texttt{globalSections\_iff\_*} are updated to supply the
182	  \texttt{trivAtBot} witness at \(\op\,\top\) via the \texttt{htop\_ne\_bot}
183	  helper.
184	\end{definition}
185	
186	\begin{definition}
187	
188	
189	\leanok
190	[Line bundle of a closed point]
191	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
192	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
193	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf, lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
194	  
195	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
196	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
199	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
200	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
201	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
202	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
203	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
204	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
205	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
206	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let \(X\) be a scheme. Then:
207	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor \(D\), \(\mathscr L(D)\) is an invertible
208	  % sheaf on \(X\).}"
209	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
210	  \(\mathscr L(D)\) and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
211	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
212	  closed point. Let \(\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}\) denote the
213	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(P\); by smoothness of \(C\),
214	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(\mathfrak m_P\)
215	  is generated by any uniformizer \(f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\) (an
216	  element of \(\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2\)). The Cartier
217	  divisor associated to the closed point \(P\) is the effective
218	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer \(f_P\) on an open
219	  affine neighbourhood of \(P\), and by the unit \(1\) on the complement
220	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). The \emph{line bundle of the closed point \(P\)},
221	  denoted \(\mathcal O_C(P)\), is the locally free \(\mathcal O_C\)-module
222	  of rank \(1\) defined by Hartshorne's construction \(\mathscr L(D)\)
223	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
224	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
225	  \(\mathcal K_C\) (the constant sheaf of the function field \(K(C)\) on the
226	  integral scheme \(C\)) that is generated by \(f_P^{-1} \in K(C)\) on a
227	  neighbourhood of \(P\) and by \(1 \in K(C)\) on the complement
228	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
229	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
230	  uniformizer \(f_P\) (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
231	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}\), so they generate the same
232	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule of \(\mathcal K_C\)).
233	
234	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
235	  \(\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C\) denote the ideal sheaf of the
236	  closed point \(P\) (locally generated by the uniformizer \(f_P\) near
237	  \(P\), and equal to \(\mathcal O_C\) on the complement). Then
238	  \(\mathcal I_P\) is the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(-P)\) associated to the
239	  Weil divisor \(-[P]\), and \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
240	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-linear dual:
241	  \[
242	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
243	    \mathcal O_C)
244	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
245	  \]
246	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
247	  ``subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\)'' description above is the one used in
248	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
249	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
250	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
251	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
252	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
253	
254	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
255	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
256	  scheme \(C\), a point \(P : C\), and a closedness witness
257	  \(\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}\) (the same input shape as
258	  REF), and returns an invertible
259	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module \(\mathcal O_C(P)\). The well-definedness as an
260	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
261	  smoothness of \(C\) (delivering the DVR stalk at \(P\)) and the standard
262	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
263	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
264	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
265	  closed plus dimension-\(1\)-integral delivers \(\mathrm{codim}_C \{P\} = 1\)
266	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
267	\end{definition}
268	
269	\begin{definition}
270	
271	\leanok
272	[Inclusion of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into the function field]
273	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}
274	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
275	  
276	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
277	  % subsheaf of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\); the
278	  % canonical inclusion \(\mathscr L(D) \hookrightarrow \mathscr K\) is part
279	  % of the construction) (read from
280	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
281	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
282	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
283	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
284	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
285	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
286	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
287	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
288	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
289	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\)
290	  as a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\)).}
291	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
292	  with \(C\) integral, and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. The line bundle
293	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF is by
294	  construction a sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient
295	  rings \(\mathcal K_C\). Since \(C\) is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the
296	  constant sheaf at the function field \(K(C)\), and taking global sections
297	  of the structural inclusion \(\mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow \mathcal K_C\)
298	  produces a canonical \(\bar k\)-linear map
299	  \[
300	    \iota \;\colon\; H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
301	      \;\longrightarrow\; H^0(C, \mathcal K_C) \;\cong\; K(C)
302	  \]
303	  sending a global section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to the rational
304	  function on \(C\) obtained by composing \(s\) with the inclusion of
305	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into \(\mathcal K_C\) at each point. The map \(\iota\) is
306	  injective (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module $\mathcal O_C(P) \subset
307	  \mathcal K_C\( is torsion-free of rank \)1$, so taking global sections
308	  preserves injectivity on the integral scheme \(C\)).

323	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}
326	
327	
328	\leanok
329	[Global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) as rational functions with a
330	controlled pole]
331	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
335	  % divisors linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) are the divisors of zeros of
336	  % global sections of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\), with the explicit
337	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
338	  % \(f\) satisfying \((f) \geq -D_0\)) (read from
339	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
340	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let \(X\) be a nonsingular
341	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field \(k\). Let
342	  % \(D_0\) be a divisor on \(X\) and let \(\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)\)
343	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
344	  % (a) for each nonzero \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\), the divisor of
345	  % zeros \((s)_0\) is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\);
346	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) is \((s)_0\)
347	  % for some \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\); and
348	  % (c) two sections \(s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\) have the same
349	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a \(\lambda \in k^*\) such
350	  % that \(s' = \lambda s\).}"
351	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If \(D \geq 0\) and \(D = D_0 + (f)\), then
352	  % \((f) \geq -D_0\). Thus \(f\) gives a global section of
353	  % \(\mathscr L(D_0)\) whose divisor of zeros is \(D\)."
354	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
355	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
356	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\), let \(P \in C\) be a
357	  closed point, and let \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) be the line bundle of
358	  REF. The \(\bar k\)-vector space of
359	  global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is naturally identified with the
360	  Riemann--Roch space
361	  \[
362	    L([P]) \;:=\;
363	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
364	    \text{ in } \mathrm{Div}(C)\bigr\}
365	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
366	  \]
367	  Equivalently, the order conditions
368	  \[
369	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
370	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
371	    \qquad
372	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1
373	  \]
374	  on \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) are equivalent to the existence of a global
375	  section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) whose image \(\iota(s)\) under
376	  the canonical inclusion $\iota : \mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
377	  \mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\( equals \)f\( (the iff binds \)s\( to \)f$
378	  explicitly — vacuous-in-\(f\) readings such as ``some nonzero global
379	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
380	
381	  Under this identification, the inclusion
382	  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of constant functions
383	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
384	  \mathcal O_C(P)\( exhibiting \)\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
385	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor \([P]\).
386	\end{lemma}
387	
388	\begin{proof}
389	  
390	  
391	  
392	  By construction, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
393	  the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathcal K_C\) generated locally by
394	  \(f_P^{-1}\) near \(P\) and by \(1\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), where \(f_P\) is
395	  a uniformizer at \(P\) (REF). Since \(C\)
396	  is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the constant sheaf at the function field
397	  \(K(C)\), and a global section of any sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module
398	  \(\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C\) is precisely an element of \(K(C)\)
399	  whose germ at every point lies in \(\mathcal F\) at that point.
400	  Therefore \(f \in K(C)\) is a global section of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) if
401	  and only if:
402	
403	  \begin{itemize}
404	    \item For every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\), the germ of
405	      \(f\) at \(Q\) lies in \(\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}\)
406	      (the structure sheaf, since \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) agrees with
407	      \(\mathcal O_C\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\)); equivalently,
408	      \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\).
409	    \item At the closed point \(P\), the germ of \(f\) at \(P\) lies in
410	      \(\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}\);
411	      equivalently, \(f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\), i.e.\
412	      \(\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\).
413	  \end{itemize}
414	  In divisorial language (REF,
415	  REF), the two conditions combine to
416	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
417	  \(L([P])\). The zero element of \(K(C)\) is included by convention as the
418	  zero global section.
419	
420	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
421	  taking \(D_0 = [P]\) effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
422	  rational function \(f\) with \((f) \geq -D_0\) defines a global section
423	  of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\). The condition \((f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]\) is exactly
424	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\). The compatibility with the constant inclusion
425	  follows by taking \(f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}\): then \(\div(1) = 0\), so
426	  \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), and the corresponding section of
427	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
428	  \cong \bar k\( under the structural inclusion \)\mathcal O_C
429	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
430	\end{proof}

432	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
433	
434	\begin{lemma}
435	
436	
437	\leanok
438	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
439	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
440	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
441	  
442	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
443	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
444	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
445	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
446	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
447	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
448	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
449	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
450	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
451	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
452	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
453	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
454	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
455	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
456	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
457	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
458	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
459	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
460	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
461	  \[
462	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
463	  \]
464	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
465	\end{lemma}
466	
467	\begin{proof}
468	  
469	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
470	  exact sequence
471	  \[
472	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
473	  \]
474	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
475	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
476	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
477	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
478	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
479	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
480	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
481	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
482	  \[
483	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
484	  \]
485	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
486	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
487	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
488	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
489	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
490	  \begin{multline*}
491	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
492	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
493	  \end{multline*}
494	  Two ingredients close the proof:
495	
496	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
497	  hypothesis: by REF,
498	  \[
499	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
500	  \]
501	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
502	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
503	
504	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
505	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
506	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
507	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
508	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
509	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
510	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
511	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
512	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
513	
514	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
515	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
516	  \[
517	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
518	  \]
519	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
520	
521	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
522	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
523	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
524	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
525	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
526	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
527	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
528	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
529	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
530	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
531	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
532	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
533	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
534	  complex degenerates).
535	\end{proof}

537	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
538	
539	% NOTE (iter-194 reviewer): The Lean body is now structurally
540	% sorry-free (closed transitively through two named substrate
541	% helpers). The substrate helpers `h0_sub_h1_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_eq_two`
542	% (the Int-valued $H^0 - H^1 = 2$ \(\chi\)-bridge; gated on
543	% `OcOfD.lean` STRUCTURALLY BLOCKED + `RRFormula.lean` $\chi$-additivity)
544	% and `h1_vanishing_genusZero` (gated on Lane H \texttt{H1Vanishing.lean}
545	% substrate) carry the remaining typed sorries.  Future consumers
546	% reading the body should consult those.
547	\begin{theorem}
548	
549	
550	\leanok
551	[\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
552	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
553	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
554	  
555	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
556	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with \(D = [P]\): $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
557	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
558	  % \(H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0\) (read from
559	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
560	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
561	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
562	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
563	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
564	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
565	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
566	  % is the Euler characteristic
567	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
568	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
569	  \(\chi\)-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
570	  genus-\(0\) specialisation chain).}
571	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
572	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
573	  \[
574	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
575	  \]
576	\end{theorem}
577	
578	\begin{proof}
579	  
580	  
581	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
582	  REF of \texttt{RR.2} to
583	  the divisor \(D = [P]\) of REF. The
584	  degree of \([P]\) is \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
585	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), and the
586	  genus hypothesis \(g(C) = 0\) (REF) gives
587	  \[
588	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
589	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
590	  \]
591	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF as a two-term Euler
592	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
593	  \[
594	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
595	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
596	    \;=\; 2.
597	  \]
598	  By
599	  REF, the second
600	  term vanishes:
601	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\). Substituting yields
602	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), as required.
603	
604	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
605	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
606	  REF computes
607	  the dimension of \(H^0\) in one stroke without invoking
608	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
609	  \[
610	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
611	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
612	  \]
613	  together with \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\)
614	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: \(H^0\) of the structure sheaf on a proper
615	  geometrically irreducible variety over \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)),
616	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) (skyscraper at a \(\bar k\)-rational
617	  point), and \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) (genus-\(0\)), the connecting
618	  map \(H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) has zero codomain and the
619	  segment yields the short exact sequence
620	  \[
621	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
622	  \]
623	  of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
624	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
625	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
626	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
627	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
628	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
629	\end{proof}

661	
662	\begin{lemma}
663	[Sub-claim (a): \(\texttt{toFunctionField}\) is \(\bar k\)-linearly
664	injective, hence preserves non-zero-ness of sections]
665	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}
666	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField_injective}
667	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField,
668	        def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf,
669	        def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
670	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible integral curve
671	  over \(\bar k\) and let \(P\in C\) be a closed point with coheight
672	  witness \(h_{P\mathrm{coh}}\colon \mathrm{coheight}(P)=1\). Then the
673	  \(\bar k\)-linear map
674	  \[
675	    \iota \;:=\;
676	    \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}\,P\,h_P\,h_{P\mathrm{coh}}
677	    \;\colon\;
678	    H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;\longrightarrow\; K(C)
679	  \]
680	  of \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField} is injective.
681	  In particular, for any global section \(s\in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\)
682	  with \(s\ne 0\), the rational function \(f:=\iota(s)\in K(C)\) is
683	  non-zero.
684	\end{lemma}
685	
686	\begin{proof}
687	  \uses{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff,
688	        def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
689	  The map \(\iota\) factors as the composition of four \(\bar k\)-linear
690	  isomorphisms and one final injective \(\bar k\)-linear map; each
691	  factor's injectivity is delivered by existing Mathlib or
692	  project-substrate hooks. Concretely, on the integral scheme \(C\),
693	  \(\iota\) equals the composition:
694	  \begin{enumerate}
695	    \item \(\texttt{HModule\_zero\_linearEquiv}_{\bar k,\,\mathcal O_C(P)}\):
696	      the project's $\bar k$-linear isomorphism
697	      $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \texttt{Scheme.HModule}\,
698	      \bar k\,\mathcal O_C(P)\,0
699	        \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
700	        \Hom_{\Sh}(\mathbf 1, \mathcal O_C(P))$
701	      with \(\mathbf 1\) the constant sheaf of \(\bar k\) on \(C\). This
702	      bridge is the project-substrate
703	      \texttt{Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK.lean} entry
704	      \texttt{HModule\_zero\_linearEquiv}.
705	    \item \texttt{sheafToPresheaf.map}: the fully faithful functor
706	      \(\Sheaf \to \Psh\) on sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules sends the
707	      sheaf-hom of layer~(1) to the underlying presheaf-hom; full
708	      faithfulness is Mathlib's
709	      \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.fullyFaithfulSheafToPresheaf}, so
710	      this step is injective on hom-sets.
711	    \item \(\texttt{constantSheafAdj}_{\bar k,\,\mathcal O_C(P)}.\texttt{homEquiv}\):
712	      Mathlib's constant-sheaf-adjunction
713	      \(\texttt{constantSheaf} \dashv \Gamma\) supplies the
714	      \(\bar k\)-linear isomorphism
715	      $\Hom_{\Psh}(\mathbf 1_{\mathrm{psh}}, \mathcal O_C(P).\val)
716	       \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
717	       \Hom_{\ModuleCat\,\bar k}(\bar k, \mathcal O_C(P).\val(\top))$.
718	    \item Evaluation at the generator \(1\in\bar k\): the canonical
719	      \(\bar k\)-linear isomorphism
720	      $\Hom_{\ModuleCat\,\bar k}(\bar k, M)
721	       \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
722	       M, \qquad \varphi \mapsto \varphi(1),$
723	      with \(M:=\mathcal O_C(P).\val(\top)\) the global-section module
724	      of \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}. The
725	      Mathlib name at the project's pinned commit may be
726	      \texttt{LinearMap.applyOneEquiv} or its
727	      \texttt{LinearEquiv.fromBaseRing} variant; the prover hand-rolls
728	      a 5-line \texttt{LinearEquiv} if the exact name has been
729	      renamed upstream.
730	    \item \texttt{Subtype.val}: the carrier sheaf
731	      \(\mathcal O_C(P).\val(\top) \;=\;
732	      \texttt{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}\,P\,h_{P\mathrm{coh}}(\op\,\top)\)
733	      is a \(\bar k\)-submodule of the function field \(K(C)\)
734	      (\cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}); the
735	      coercion \texttt{Subtype.val} to \(K(C)\) is injective.
736	  \end{enumerate}
737	  Each layer (1)--(4) is a \texttt{LinearEquiv}; layer (5) is
738	  \texttt{Function.Injective Subtype.val}. The composition is therefore
739	  an injective \(\bar k\)-linear map, and this composition is precisely
740	  the body unfolding of
741	  \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}: the iter-187
742	  refactor of \texttt{toFunctionField} is the formal record of this
743	  factorisation (compare the \emph{iter-187 closure} commentary at
744	  \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField} and the
745	  \texttt{globalSections\_iff\_mpr} proof at line~1024 of
746	  \texttt{OCofP.lean}, where the same chain is unfolded in the
747	  reverse direction). The non-zero-transfer conclusion is immediate:
748	  if \(\iota(s)=0\) and \(s\ne 0\), then \(\iota\) has a non-trivial
749	  kernel, contradicting injectivity; equivalently, applying
750	  injectivity to \(\iota(s)=0=\iota(0)\) (using
751	  \texttt{htF\_zero}, already in scope at line~1364 of the helper
752	  body) gives \(s=0\), contradicting the iter-195 hypothesis
753	  \(s\notin \bar k\cdot s_1\) (which is strictly stronger than
754	  \(s\ne 0\)).
755	
756	  \emph{Estimated formalisation footprint.} \(\sim 30\)--\(50\)
757	  Lean LOC: layer~(1) is one line (named bridge); layer~(2) is
758	  \(\sim 5\) LOC; layer~(3) is \(\sim 5\)--\(10\) LOC; layer~(4) is
759	  \(\sim 5\)--\(10\) LOC (hand-roll if necessary); layer~(5) is one
760	  line (\texttt{Subtype.val\_injective}); the composition and the
761	  final \(s\ne 0\Rightarrow\iota(s)\ne 0\) wrap each add 5--10 LOC.
762	\end{proof}

764	\begin{lemma}
765	[Sub-claim (b): order conditions on \(\iota(s)\) from
766	\texttt{globalSections\_iff\_mpr}]
767	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_order_conditions_of_globalSection}
768	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.order_conditions_of_globalSection}

770	        lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}
771	  Let \(C, P, h_P, h_{P\mathrm{coh}}\) be as in

816	\begin{lemma}
817	[Sub-claim (c): principal divisor of a non-constant rational function
818	on a complete curve is non-zero]
819	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_principal_ne_zero_of_nonconstant}
820	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_ne_zero_of_nonconstant}
821	  \uses{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective,
822	        lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_order_conditions_of_globalSection}
823	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible integral curve
824	  over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(P\in C\) be a
825	  closed point with coheight witness, let
826	  \(s\in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) be a global section satisfying
827	  \(s\notin \bar k\cdot s_1\) (with \(s_1\) the distinguished constant
828	  section, iter-195 setup line~1355 of \texttt{OCofP.lean}), and set
829	  \(f:=\iota(s)\) with \(h_f\colon f\ne 0\) supplied by
830	  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}. Then
831	  the principal Weil divisor of \(f\) is non-zero:
832	  \[
833	    \texttt{Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}\,(X:=C.\mathrm{left})\,f\,h_f
834	    \;\ne\;
835	    0 \quad \text{in } \mathrm{Div}(C.\mathrm{left}).
836	  \]
837	\end{lemma}
838	
839	\begin{proof}
840	  \uses{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}
841	  Argue by the contrapositive. Suppose for contradiction that
842	  \(\texttt{principal}\,f\,h_f = 0\). The principal divisor is
843	  \(\sum_Q \ord_Q(f)\cdot [Q]\), summed over prime divisors
844	  \(Q\in C.\mathrm{PrimeDivisor}\); the vanishing of the principal
845	  divisor is therefore equivalent to \(\ord_Q(f)=0\) for every prime
846	  divisor \(Q\) of \(C\). Hence \(f\) has no zeros and no poles on
847	  \(C\), and the inclusion
848	  \(\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow \mathcal K_C\) places \(f\) inside
849	  the subsheaf of regular functions on \(C\), i.e.\
850	  \(f\in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)\). On a complete geometrically
851	  irreducible integral scheme over the algebraically closed field
852	  \(\bar k\) (in particular: smooth proper integral curve), the
853	  ring of global sections of the structure sheaf is exactly the base
854	  field,
855	  \(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) (Hartshorne~I.3.4 / Stacks
856	  Project tag \texttt{01XU}); the non-vanishing \(f\ne 0\) plus the

857	  fact that all elements of \(\mathcal O_C^{\times}(\top)\) coincide
858	  with \(\bar k^{\times}\) (the units refinement, Stacks Project tag
859	  \texttt{02P0}) places \(f\in \bar k^{\times}\subset K(C)\).
860	
861	  We now derive a contradiction from the hypothesis
862	  \(s\notin\bar k\cdot s_1\). Set \(c:=f\in\bar k\), so
863	  \(\iota(s) - c \cdot 1 = 0\) in \(K(C)\), i.e.\
864	  \(\iota(s) - c \cdot \iota(s_1) = 0\) (since \(\iota(s_1)=1\), the
865	  defining equation \texttt{hs1 : toFunctionField s\(_1\) = 1} at
866	  line~1355 of \texttt{OCofP.lean}). By the \(\bar k\)-linearity of
867	  \(\iota\) (witnesses \texttt{htF\_smul} at line~1369 and
868	  \texttt{htF\_add} at line~1378 of the iter-195 helper body),
869	  \(\iota(s - c\cdot s_1) = 0\). By
870	  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}, the
871	  map \(\iota\) is injective, so \(s - c\cdot s_1 = 0\), i.e.\
872	  \(s = c\cdot s_1 \in \bar k\cdot s_1\), contradicting the iter-195
873	  hypothesis \(s\notin\bar k\cdot s_1\). Hence \(\texttt{principal}\,
874	  f\,h_f \ne 0\), as required.
875	
876	  \emph{Mathlib substrate consumed.} The statement
877	  ``global sections of \(\mathcal O_C\) on a complete integral curve
878	  over \(\bar k\) are exactly \(\bar k\)'' is the Hartshorne~I.3.4 /
879	  Stacks~\texttt{01XU} input; its units refinement (Stacks~\texttt{02P0})
880	  is direct since the constants \(\bar k\) form a field. At the
881	  pinned Mathlib commit the relevant entry-point is the
882	  project's existing function-field-of-curve identification + the
883	  proper + integral + algebraically-closed-base typeclass package
884	  available in the helper's local context. If a self-contained
885	  project-side lemma \texttt{Scheme.functionField\_of\_complete\_curve\_const}
886	  (or equivalent) is not yet available, the iter-196 prover lands a
887	  small named helper for this fact (estimated \(\sim 15\)--\(25\) LOC
888	  for the Hartshorne~I.3.4 statement) and consumes it here.
889	
890	  \emph{Estimated formalisation footprint.} \(\sim 30\)--\(50\) Lean
891	  LOC for the contrapositive setup, the
892	  ``no-zeros-or-poles \(\Rightarrow\) regular'' step, the constants
893	  identification, and the final linear-independence contradiction.
894	  The kbar-linearity witnesses (\texttt{htF\_smul},
895	  \texttt{htF\_add}, \texttt{htF\_zero}) are already in scope
896	  (lines~1364--1387 of \texttt{OCofP.lean}); the injectivity hypothesis
897	  is consumed from
898	  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}.
899	\end{proof}

901	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
902	
903	\begin{corollary}
904	
905	
906	\leanok
907	[A genus-\(0\) curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
908	single simple pole]
909	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
910	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
911	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
912	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
913	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
914	  
915	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
916	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
917	  % point on a genus-\(0\) curve, as a direct consequence of the
918	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
919	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
920	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let \(X\) be a curve, and let \(P \in X\) be a
921	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
922	  % K(X)\(, which is regular everywhere except at \)P$."
923	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
924	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
925	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
926	  there exists a non-constant rational function \(f \in K(C)\) such that:
927	  \begin{itemize}
928	    \item \(f\) is regular on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
929	      for every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\);
930	    \item \(f\) has at most a simple pole at \(P\), i.e.\
931	      \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\);
932	    \item \(f \notin \bar k\), equivalently
933	      \(\div(f) \neq 0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\).
934	  \end{itemize}
935	  In particular, the pair \((1, f)\) spans
936	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
937	\end{corollary}
938	
939	\begin{proof}
940	  
941	  
942	  By
943	  REF,
944	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\). The constant subspace
945	  \(\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\), obtained from the
946	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
947	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
948	  \(f = 1\), with \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), under the
949	  identification of
950	  REF), is a one-dimensional
951	  \(\bar k\)-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension \(2\),
952	  the quotient \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1\) is a
953	  non-zero one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector space; any lift to
954	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of a non-zero element of the quotient is
955	  an element of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1\).
956	
957	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
958	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
959	  REF the section
960	  \(s\) corresponds to a rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)
961	  satisfying \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), equivalently \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
962	  for every \(Q \neq P\) and \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\). The choice
963	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) means \(f\) is not the image of a constant,
964	  so \(f \notin \bar k\) (every non-zero element of
965	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k\) corresponds, under the
966	  identification, to a constant element of \(K(C)\)). The three bullets
967	  of the statement are now immediate.
968	
969	  Spanning. Linear independence of \(1\) and \(f\) over \(\bar k\) in
970	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) is the same condition as
971	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) above; combined with the dimension count
972	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), the pair \((1, f)\) is a
973	  \(\bar k\)-basis of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
974	
975	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
976	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
977	  returns an element \(f\) of the project's function-field carrier
978	  \(K(C)\) together with proofs of regularity on \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and
979	  the simple-pole bound at \(P\), packaged in the same shape as
980	  REF's output. The
981	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
982	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
983	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
984	\end{proof}

19	through:

21	- `RR.1` (`WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil divisor group `Div(C)` and the
22	  degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`.

24	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` and the Riemann–Roch dimension formula
25	  `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` in genus `0`.
26	- **`RR.3` (this file)**: the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point
27	  `P ∈ C`, its `k̄`-vector space of global sections as the

30	  `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2`, and the existence of a non-constant
31	  rational function `f ∈ K(C)` with at most a simple pole at `P`.
32	- `RR.4` (`RationalCurveIso.lean`, future): the "two-section
33	  ⇒ `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` ⇒ `≅ ℙ¹`" classification.

112	/-! ## §1. The line bundle of a closed point on a smooth proper curve
113	
114	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` and a closed
115	point `P ∈ C`, the local ring `𝒪_{C,P}` is a DVR with maximal ideal
116	generated by a uniformiser `f_P`. Hartshorne's construction `ℒ(D)`
117	(II §6 p. 144) applied to the Cartier divisor `[P]` (locally cut out by
118	`f_P` near `P`, by `1` elsewhere) produces the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)`:
119	the sub-`𝒪_C`-module of the function-field constant sheaf `𝒦_C ≅ K(C)`
120	generated locally by `f_P^{-1}` near `P` and by `1` on `C \ {P}`. -/
121	
122	namespace Scheme
123	
124	/-! ### Hartshorne subsheaf-of-`K_C` carrier (iter-183 Lane A scaffold)
125	
126	The substantive iter-183 contribution: a concrete per-open `Set`-valued
127	carrier of the line bundle `𝒪_C(P)`, realised directly as Hartshorne's
128	subsheaf of the function-field constant sheaf `K_C` (Hartshorne II §6
129	p. 144; analogist `ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md` Decision 3
130	`ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB`).
131	
132	A section of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U` is a rational function `f ∈ K(C)`
133	satisfying the order conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor
134	`Q ≠ P` with `Q.point ∈ U` (regularity on the complement of `P` inside
135	`U`) and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` when `P ∈ U` (at most a simple pole at `P`).
136	The construction is independent of the choice of uniformiser at `P`:
137	any two uniformisers differ by a unit, so the order-`≥ −1` condition at
138	`P` is intrinsic.
139	
140	iter-184+ will upgrade `carrierSet` to a `Submodule kbar K(C)` via the
141	closure proofs (zero / addition / kbar-scalar), bundle the result as a
142	presheaf functor (identity-on-`K(C)` restrictions, monotone in `U` via
143	`carrierSet_mono`), and discharge the sheaf property via gluing-by-
144	stalks (stalk-locality of the order conditions at each prime divisor). -/
145	
146	/-- **Carrier set** of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U : (Opens C.left)ᵒᵖ`: the
147	set of rational functions `f ∈ K(C)` satisfying the order conditions
148	`ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q` with `Q.point ∈ U.unop`,
149	`Q.point ≠ P`, and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` when `P ∈ U.unop`.
150	
151	iter-183 Lane A landed this as a concrete substantive `Set` definition;
152	no sorry. -/
153	private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet
154	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
155	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
156	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
157	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
158	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
159	    (U : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
160	    Set C.left.functionField := by
161	  let Phat : C.left.PrimeDivisor := ⟨P, hPcoh⟩
162	  haveI := Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instKrullDimLEStalk
163	    (X := C.left) Phat
164	  exact { f | (∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Q.point ∈ U.unop.1 → Q.point ≠ P →
165	              0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f) ∧
166	              (P ∈ U.unop.1 → (-1 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Phat f) }
167	
168	/-- **Monotonicity of `carrierSet` in `U`**: when `V.unop ⊆ U.unop` (i.e.
169	the open `V` is contained in the open `U`), the carrier on `U` is
170	INCLUDED in the carrier on `V` (the order conditions over the smaller
171	open `V` involve fewer prime divisors, hence are easier to satisfy).
172	
173	This is the substantive monotonicity that drives the (identity-on-`K(C)`)
174	restriction map of the would-be `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.presheaf` functor:
175	in `(Opens C.left)ᵒᵖ`, an arrow `U ⟶ V` corresponds to `V.unop ⊆ U.unop`,
176	and the restriction map `carrierSet U → carrierSet V` is the inclusion
177	delivered by this lemma.
178	
179	iter-183 Lane A landed this as the substantive monotonicity proof; no
180	sorry. -/
181	private lemma lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet_mono
182	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
183	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
184	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
185	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
186	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
187	    {U V : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ}
188	    (hUV : V.unop.1 ⊆ U.unop.1) :
189	    lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh U
190	      ⊆ lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh V := by
191	  intro f hf
192	  refine ⟨fun Q hQV hQP => hf.1 Q (hUV hQV) hQP, fun hPV => hf.2 (hUV hPV)⟩
193	
194	/-- **Nonempty top-open**: for an integral scheme `X`, the top open is
195	nonempty (its carrier is `Set.univ` and `X` itself is nonempty since
196	`IsIntegral X ⟹ IrreducibleSpace X ⟹ Nonempty X`). Supplied as an
197	instance so that the standard Mathlib `Algebra Γ(X, U) X.functionField`
198	instance fires at `U = ⊤`. -/
199	instance lineBundleAtClosedPoint.instNonemptyTopOpen
200	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] :

200	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] :
201	    Nonempty ((⊤ : X.Opens) : Scheme) :=
202	  (Scheme.Opens.nonempty_iff (U := (⊤ : X.Opens))).mpr
203	    ⟨(inferInstance : Nonempty X).some, Set.mem_univ _⟩
204	
205	/-- **`kbar`-algebra structure on the function field `K(C)`**. The
206	project's `Scheme.toModuleKSheaf.algebraSection` instance gives
207	`Algebra kbar Γ(C.left, ⊤)`, and Mathlib's standard
208	`AlgebraicGeometry.instAlgebraCarrierObjOppositeOpens...` gives
209	`Algebra Γ(C.left, ⊤) K(C)` via `germToFunctionField`. Composing the
210	two algebra maps via `RingHom.toAlgebra` produces the desired
211	`Algebra kbar K(C)` instance, which is needed for `Submodule kbar K(C)`
212	to type-check. -/
213	noncomputable instance lineBundleAtClosedPoint.instAlgebraKbarFunctionField
214	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar]
215	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsIntegral C.left] :
216	    Algebra kbar C.left.functionField := by
217	  haveI : Nonempty (⊤ : C.left.Opens) :=
218	    (AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.nonempty_iff
219	      (X := C.left) (U := (⊤ : C.left.Opens))).mpr
220	      ⟨(inferInstance : Nonempty C.left).some, Set.mem_univ _⟩
221	  exact RingHom.toAlgebra
222	    ((Scheme.germToFunctionField C.left (⊤ : C.left.Opens)).hom.comp
223	      (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf.kToSection C
224	        (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))).hom)
225	
226	/-- **Carrier submodule** of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U`: upgrade of
227	`carrierSet U` to a `Submodule kbar K(C)` via the three closure proofs
228	(`0`, `+`, `kbar • _`). The closure proofs rest on the DVR-shipped
229	`Ring.ordFrac_add` (non-archimedean inequality on the discrete valuation
230	of a regular-in-codim-1 stalk; iter-186 Step 1 upgraded the carrier class
231	so this is invocable) and `Ring.ordFrac_of_isUnit` (the scalar from
232	`kbar` becomes a unit in the stalk, preserving the order under scalar
233	multiplication).
234	
235	iter-186 Step 2 (per analogist `ocofp-carrierset-submodule-api.md`
236	Decision 2): structural skeleton with the load-bearing Mathlib lemmas in
237	place; bookkeeping `sorry`s remain for the prover phase. -/
238	private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmodule
239	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
240	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
241	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
242	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
243	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
244	    (U : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
245	    Submodule kbar C.left.functionField where
246	  carrier := lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh U
247	  zero_mem' := by
248	    -- `order Y 0 = WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0`;
249	    -- both `0 ≤ 0` and `-1 ≤ 0` hold trivially.
250	    refine ⟨fun Q _ _ => ?_, fun _ => ?_⟩
251	    · simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
252	    · simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
253	  add_mem' := by
254	    -- Case-split on `f + g = 0` (trivial via `WithZero.log_zero`), else
255	    -- apply `Ring.ordFrac_add` (DVR-shipped, Step 1 unlocks) and
256	    -- `WithZero.log` monotonicity on the nonzero part.
257	    rintro a b ⟨ha₁, ha₂⟩ ⟨hb₁, hb₂⟩
258	    have hMNZ : ∀ (Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor) (x : C.left.functionField),
259	        x ≠ 0 → Ring.ordFrac (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point) x ≠ 0 := by
260	      intro Q x hx; simp [hx]
261	    have key : ∀ (Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor) (n : ℤ),
262	        n ≤ 0 → n ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q a →
263	          n ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q b →
264	          n ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q (a + b) := by
265	      intro Q n hn haₚ hbₚ
266	      by_cases hab : a + b = 0
267	      · simpa [hab, Scheme.RationalMap.order] using hn
268	      by_cases hae : a = 0
269	      · rw [hae, zero_add]; exact hbₚ
270	      by_cases hbe : b = 0
271	      · rw [hbe, add_zero]; exact haₚ
272	      set R := C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point
273	      have hoa : Ring.ordFrac R a ≠ 0 := hMNZ Q a hae
274	      have hob : Ring.ordFrac R b ≠ 0 := hMNZ Q b hbe
275	      have hoab : Ring.ordFrac R (a + b) ≠ 0 := hMNZ Q _ hab
276	      have hmin : Ring.ordFrac R a ⊓ Ring.ordFrac R b ≤ Ring.ordFrac R (a + b) :=
277	        Ring.ordFrac_add (R := R) a b hab
278	      have hlog : (Ring.ordFrac R a ⊓ Ring.ordFrac R b).log ≤
279	          Scheme.RationalMap.order Q (a + b) := by
280	        rcases min_cases (Ring.ordFrac R a) (Ring.ordFrac R b) with
281	            ⟨heq, _⟩ | ⟨heq, _⟩
282	        · rw [heq]; exact (WithZero.log_le_log hoa hoab).mpr (heq ▸ hmin)
283	        · rw [heq]; exact (WithZero.log_le_log hob hoab).mpr (heq ▸ hmin)
284	      have hminbd : n ≤ (Ring.ordFrac R a ⊓ Ring.ordFrac R b).log := by
285	        rcases min_cases (Ring.ordFrac R a) (Ring.ordFrac R b) with
286	            ⟨heq, _⟩ | ⟨heq, _⟩
287	        · rw [heq]; exact haₚ
288	        · rw [heq]; exact hbₚ
289	      linarith
290	    refine ⟨fun Q hQU hQP => key Q 0 le_rfl (ha₁ Q hQU hQP) (hb₁ Q hQU hQP),
291	      fun hPU => key ⟨P, hPcoh⟩ (-1) (by norm_num) (ha₂ hPU) (hb₂ hPU)⟩
292	  smul_mem' := by
293	    -- For `c : kbar`, the scalar action `c • f` on `K(C)` factors through
294	    -- `algebraMap kbar K(C)`. For `c ≠ 0`, `algebraMap kbar K(C) c` lifts to
295	    -- a nonzero `β` in every stalk via the germ map; `Ring.ordFrac_ge_one_of_ne_zero`
296	    -- on `β` then gives `order Q (algebraMap c) ≥ 0`, hence `c•x ∈ carrierSet`.
297	    intro c x ⟨hx₁, hx₂⟩
298	    rcases eq_or_ne c 0 with rfl | hc
299	    · -- `c = 0` ⇒ `0 • x = 0` ∈ carrierSet via `zero_mem'`.
300	      simp only [zero_smul]
301	      refine ⟨fun Q _ _ => ?_, fun _ => ?_⟩ <;> simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
302	    rcases eq_or_ne x 0 with rfl | hx_ne
303	    · -- `x = 0` ⇒ `c • 0 = 0` ∈ carrierSet.
304	      simp only [smul_zero]
305	      refine ⟨fun Q _ _ => ?_, fun _ => ?_⟩ <;> simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]

310	      have hinj := FaithfulSMul.algebraMap_injective kbar C.left.functionField

314	      intro Q y hy; simp [hy]

316	        0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q
317	              (algebraMap kbar C.left.functionField c) := by
318	      intro Q

320	      let β : R := (C.left.presheaf.germ (⊤ : C.left.Opens) Q.point trivial).hom
321	        ((Scheme.toModuleKSheaf.kToSection C

325	        change ((Scheme.germToFunctionField C.left (⊤ : C.left.Opens)).hom
326	            ((Scheme.toModuleKSheaf.kToSection C
327	                (Opposite.op (⊤ : C.left.Opens))).hom c)) =
328	            (C.left.presheaf.stalkSpecializes
329	              ((genericPoint_spec C.left).specializes trivial)).hom β

331	          (h := (genericPoint_spec C.left).specializes trivial)]

419	/-- **Type-level Subfunctor presentation of the carrier** (iter-189 Subfunctor
420	restructure). The carrier of `𝒪_C(P)` viewed as a `CategoryTheory.Subfunctor` of
421	the Type-valued presheaf `TopCat.presheafToType C.left.toTopCat C.left.functionField`
422	(the presheaf of arbitrary functions to the function field `K(C)`). A section over
423	an open `U` is a function `g : U.unop → K(C)` that is constant with value
424	`f ∈ carrierSubmoduleSheaf U`.

426	This Subfunctor packages the carrier conditions uniformly:
427	- At `U ≠ ⊥`: a section is a constant function valued in
428	  `carrierSubmoduleSheaf U = carrierSubmodule U` (so the order conditions hold).
429	- At `U = ⊥`: the unique empty function (witnessed by `f = 0 ∈ ⊥`).

432	sheaf `TopCat.Presheaf.toType_isSheaf` for `presheafToType`, reduces the sheaf
433	condition for the (type-valued shadow of the) carrier to a stalk-locality check:
434	every section of `presheafToType K(C)` whose sieve-of-section is covering already
435	lies in the Subfunctor. On the irreducible scheme `C.left`, this stalk-locality
436	holds because any two non-empty opens intersect, forcing constant-function gluing
437	to agree on overlaps, and the per-prime-divisor order conditions transfer from
438	each open in the cover to their union pointwise. -/

440	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
441	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
442	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
443	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
444	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1) :
445	    CategoryTheory.Subfunctor
446	      (TopCat.presheafToType C.left.toTopCat C.left.functionField) where
447	  obj U := { g : U.unop → C.left.functionField |
448	    ∃ f : C.left.functionField,
449	      f ∈ lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmoduleSheaf P hPcoh U ∧
450	        g = fun _ => f }
451	  map := by
452	    classical
453	    intro U V i g hg
454	    obtain ⟨f, hf, hgf⟩ := hg
455	    by_cases hV : V.unop = (⊥ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)
456	    · -- `V = ⊥`: `V.unop` is the empty type. Any function with empty domain works;
457	      -- use the witness `f' = 0 ∈ carrierSubmoduleSheaf ⊥ = ⊥`.
458	      refine ⟨0, ?_, ?_⟩
459	      · exact (lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmoduleSheaf P hPcoh V).zero_mem
460	      · funext x

893	curve, the condition `div(f) + [P] ≥ 0` rewrites coordinate-wise as
894	`ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`. -/

900	`s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` whose image under the canonical inclusion

903	affine cover witnessing the Cartier-divisor structure of `[P]`: on a
904	neighbourhood `U_i` of `P` the section is `f · f_P` (in
905	`𝒪_C(P)(U_i) = f_P⁻¹ · 𝒪_C(U_i)`, this is `(f · f_P) · f_P⁻¹ = f`);
906	on the complement `C ∖ {P}` the section is `f` directly (the order

998	Given a global section `s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` whose image under

1001

1058	/-- **Global sections of `𝒪_C(P)` as rational functions with controlled
1059	pole at `P`** (Hartshorne II §7 Proposition 7.7, p. 157).
1060	
1061	For a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×`, the following are
1062	equivalent:
1063	
1064	* there exists a global section `s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` whose image under
1065	  the canonical inclusion `𝒪_C(P) ↪ 𝒦_C ≅ K(C)` equals `f` (formally,
1066	  `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField P hP s = f`);
1067	* the order conditions hold: `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor
1068	  `Q ∈ C.PrimeDivisor` whose generic point is not `P`, and
1069	  `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` (where the latter is read off the prime divisor
1070	  `⟨P, h⟩` with `h : Order.coheight P = 1` the codimension-one witness
1071	  automatic for a closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme).

132	H⁰ bridge `HModule_zero_linearEquiv : HModule k F 0 ≃ₗ[k] ((constantSheaf J _).obj
133	(ModuleCat.of k k) ⟶ F)` identifies the degree-zero cohomology with a `k`-linear Hom
134	group; Mathlib's `Module.Finite.equiv` then transports `Module.Finite k`-ness across.
135	The `.symm` is required: iter-010's bridge has `HModule k F 0` on the LHS and the

6	module

8	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Preadditive.Yoneda.Basic
9	
10	/-!
11	# Adjunctions between additive functors.
12	
13	This provides some results and constructions for adjunctions between functors on
14	preadditive categories:
15	* If one of the adjoint functors is additive, so is the other.
16	* If one of the adjoint functors is additive, the equivalence `Adjunction.homEquiv` lifts to
17	  an additive equivalence `Adjunction.homAddEquiv`.
18	* We also give a version of this additive equivalence as an isomorphism of `preadditiveYoneda`
19	  functors (analogous to `Adjunction.compYonedaIso`), in `Adjunction.compPreadditiveYonedaIso`.
20	
21	-/
22	
23	@[expose] public section
24	
25	universe u₁ u₂ v₁ v₂
26	
27	namespace CategoryTheory
28	
29	namespace Adjunction
30	
31	open CategoryTheory Category Functor
32	
33	variable {C : Type u₁} {D : Type u₂} [Category.{v₁} C] [Category.{v₂} D] [Preadditive C]
34	  [Preadditive D] {F : C ⥤ D} {G : D ⥤ C} (adj : F ⊣ G)
35	
36	include adj
37	
38	lemma right_adjoint_additive [F.Additive] : G.Additive where
39	  map_add {X Y} f g := (adj.homEquiv _ _).symm.injective (by simp [homEquiv_counit])
40	
41	lemma left_adjoint_additive [G.Additive] : F.Additive where
42	  map_add {X Y} f g := (adj.homEquiv _ _).injective (by simp [homEquiv_unit])
43	
44	variable [F.Additive]
45	
46	/-- If we have an adjunction `adj : F ⊣ G` of functors between preadditive categories,
47	and if `F` is additive, then the hom set equivalence upgrades to an `AddEquiv`.
48	Note that `F` is additive if and only if `G` is, by `Adjunction.right_adjoint_additive` and
49	`Adjunction.left_adjoint_additive`.
50	-/
51	def homAddEquiv (X : C) (Y : D) : AddEquiv (F.obj X ⟶ Y) (X ⟶ G.obj Y) :=
52	  { adj.homEquiv _ _ with
53	    map_add' _ _ := by
54	      have := adj.right_adjoint_additive
55	      simp [homEquiv_apply] }
56	
57	@[simp]
58	lemma homAddEquiv_apply (X : C) (Y : D) (f : F.obj X ⟶ Y) :
59	    adj.homAddEquiv X Y f = adj.homEquiv X Y f := rfl

102	/-- If we have an adjunction `adj : F ⊣ G` of functors between preadditive categories,
103	and if `F` is additive, then the hom set equivalence upgrades to an isomorphism between
104	`G ⋙ preadditiveYoneda` and `preadditiveYoneda ⋙ F`, once we throw in the necessary
105	universe lifting functors.
106	Note that `F` is additive if and only if `G` is, by `Adjunction.right_adjoint_additive` and
107	`Adjunction.left_adjoint_additive`.

109	def compPreadditiveYonedaIso :
110	    G ⋙ preadditiveYoneda ⋙ (whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj AddCommGrpCat.uliftFunctor.{max v₁ v₂} ≅
111	      preadditiveYoneda ⋙ (whiskeringLeft _ _ _).obj F.op ⋙

9	
10	/-!
11	# The line bundle `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point and its global sections (RR.3)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.3** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
14	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically irreducible
15	genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in
16	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
17	
18	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ↦ `ℙ¹` classification routes
19	through:
20	
21	- `RR.1` (`WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil divisor group `Div(C)` and the
22	  degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`.
23	- `RR.2` (`RRFormula.lean`): the Euler-characteristic identity
24	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` and the Riemann–Roch dimension formula
25	  `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` in genus `0`.
26	- **`RR.3` (this file)**: the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point
27	  `P ∈ C`, its `k̄`-vector space of global sections as the
28	  Riemann–Roch space `L([P])`, the `H¹`-vanishing
29	  `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 0` on a genus-`0` curve, the dimension formula
30	  `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2`, and the existence of a non-constant
31	  rational function `f ∈ K(C)` with at most a simple pole at `P`.
32	- `RR.4` (`RationalCurveIso.lean`, future): the "two-section
33	  ⇒ `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` ⇒ `≅ ℙ¹`" classification.
34

58	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint` — the invertible
59	   sheaf `𝒪_C(P)` associated to a closed point `P` on a smooth proper
60	   curve `C / k̄`.

62	   — the identification of global sections of `𝒪_C(P)` with the

112	/-! ## §1. The line bundle of a closed point on a smooth proper curve
113	
114	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` and a closed
115	point `P ∈ C`, the local ring `𝒪_{C,P}` is a DVR with maximal ideal
116	generated by a uniformiser `f_P`. Hartshorne's construction `ℒ(D)`
117	(II §6 p. 144) applied to the Cartier divisor `[P]` (locally cut out by
118	`f_P` near `P`, by `1` elsewhere) produces the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)`:
119	the sub-`𝒪_C`-module of the function-field constant sheaf `𝒦_C ≅ K(C)`
120	generated locally by `f_P^{-1}` near `P` and by `1` on `C \ {P}`. -/
121	
122	namespace Scheme
123	
124	/-! ### Hartshorne subsheaf-of-`K_C` carrier (iter-183 Lane A scaffold)
125	
126	The substantive iter-183 contribution: a concrete per-open `Set`-valued
127	carrier of the line bundle `𝒪_C(P)`, realised directly as Hartshorne's
128	subsheaf of the function-field constant sheaf `K_C` (Hartshorne II §6
129	p. 144; analogist `ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md` Decision 3
130	`ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB`).
131	
132	A section of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U` is a rational function `f ∈ K(C)`
133	satisfying the order conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor
134	`Q ≠ P` with `Q.point ∈ U` (regularity on the complement of `P` inside
135	`U`) and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` when `P ∈ U` (at most a simple pole at `P`).
136	The construction is independent of the choice of uniformiser at `P`:
137	any two uniformisers differ by a unit, so the order-`≥ −1` condition at
138	`P` is intrinsic.
139	
140	iter-184+ will upgrade `carrierSet` to a `Submodule kbar K(C)` via the
141	closure proofs (zero / addition / kbar-scalar), bundle the result as a
142	presheaf functor (identity-on-`K(C)` restrictions, monotone in `U` via
143	`carrierSet_mono`), and discharge the sheaf property via gluing-by-
144	stalks (stalk-locality of the order conditions at each prime divisor). -/
145	
146	/-- **Carrier set** of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U : (Opens C.left)ᵒᵖ`: the
147	set of rational functions `f ∈ K(C)` satisfying the order conditions
148	`ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q` with `Q.point ∈ U.unop`,
149	`Q.point ≠ P`, and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` when `P ∈ U.unop`.
150	
151	iter-183 Lane A landed this as a concrete substantive `Set` definition;
152	no sorry. -/
153	private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet
154	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
155	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
156	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
157	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
158	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
159	    (U : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
160	    Set C.left.functionField := by
161	  let Phat : C.left.PrimeDivisor := ⟨P, hPcoh⟩
162	  haveI := Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instKrullDimLEStalk
163	    (X := C.left) Phat
164	  exact { f | (∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Q.point ∈ U.unop.1 → Q.point ≠ P →
165	              0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f) ∧
166	              (P ∈ U.unop.1 → (-1 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Phat f) }
167	
168	/-- **Monotonicity of `carrierSet` in `U`**: when `V.unop ⊆ U.unop` (i.e.
169	the open `V` is contained in the open `U`), the carrier on `U` is
170	INCLUDED in the carrier on `V` (the order conditions over the smaller
171	open `V` involve fewer prime divisors, hence are easier to satisfy).
172	
173	This is the substantive monotonicity that drives the (identity-on-`K(C)`)
174	restriction map of the would-be `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.presheaf` functor:
175	in `(Opens C.left)ᵒᵖ`, an arrow `U ⟶ V` corresponds to `V.unop ⊆ U.unop`,
176	and the restriction map `carrierSet U → carrierSet V` is the inclusion
177	delivered by this lemma.
178	
179	iter-183 Lane A landed this as the substantive monotonicity proof; no
180	sorry. -/
181	private lemma lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet_mono
182	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
183	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
184	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
185	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
186	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
187	    {U V : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ}
188	    (hUV : V.unop.1 ⊆ U.unop.1) :
189	    lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh U
190	      ⊆ lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh V := by
191	  intro f hf
192	  refine ⟨fun Q hQV hQP => hf.1 Q (hUV hQV) hQP, fun hPV => hf.2 (hUV hPV)⟩
193	
194	/-- **Nonempty top-open**: for an integral scheme `X`, the top open is
195	nonempty (its carrier is `Set.univ` and `X` itself is nonempty since
196	`IsIntegral X ⟹ IrreducibleSpace X ⟹ Nonempty X`). Supplied as an
197	instance so that the standard Mathlib `Algebra Γ(X, U) X.functionField`
198	instance fires at `U = ⊤`. -/
199	instance lineBundleAtClosedPoint.instNonemptyTopOpen
200	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] :
201	    Nonempty ((⊤ : X.Opens) : Scheme) :=
202	  (Scheme.Opens.nonempty_iff (U := (⊤ : X.Opens))).mpr
203	    ⟨(inferInstance : Nonempty X).some, Set.mem_univ _⟩
204	
205	/-- **`kbar`-algebra structure on the function field `K(C)`**. The
206	project's `Scheme.toModuleKSheaf.algebraSection` instance gives
207	`Algebra kbar Γ(C.left, ⊤)`, and Mathlib's standard
208	`AlgebraicGeometry.instAlgebraCarrierObjOppositeOpens...` gives
209	`Algebra Γ(C.left, ⊤) K(C)` via `germToFunctionField`. Composing the
210	two algebra maps via `RingHom.toAlgebra` produces the desired
211	`Algebra kbar K(C)` instance, which is needed for `Submodule kbar K(C)`
212	to type-check. -/
213	noncomputable instance lineBundleAtClosedPoint.instAlgebraKbarFunctionField
214	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar]
215	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsIntegral C.left] :

889	
890	Hartshorne II §7 Proposition 7.7 identifies the global sections of
891	`ℒ(D_0)` with the rational functions `f ∈ K(X)^×` satisfying
892	`div(f) ≥ −D_0` (plus the zero section). Specialised to `D_0 = [P]` on a
893	curve, the condition `div(f) + [P] ≥ 0` rewrites coordinate-wise as
894	`ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`. -/

896	/-- **Forward direction of `globalSections_iff` (Hartshorne II.7.7(b)).**
897	
898	Given `f ∈ K(C)^×` with `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ≠ P`
899	and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`, the rational function `f` lifts to a global section
900	`s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` whose image under the canonical inclusion
901	`𝒪_C(P) ↪ 𝒦_C ≅ K(C)` equals `f`. Hartshorne's construction inside the
902	proof of Proposition 7.7(b) (p. 157) defines this section locally on the
903	affine cover witnessing the Cartier-divisor structure of `[P]`: on a
904	neighbourhood `U_i` of `P` the section is `f · f_P` (in
905	`𝒪_C(P)(U_i) = f_P⁻¹ · 𝒪_C(U_i)`, this is `(f · f_P) · f_P⁻¹ = f`);
906	on the complement `C ∖ {P}` the section is `f` directly (the order
907	conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P` guarantee `f ∈ 𝒪_C(U) = 𝒪_C(P)(U)`

1059	pole at `P`** (Hartshorne II §7 Proposition 7.7, p. 157).

1061	For a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×`, the following are
1062	equivalent:
1063	
1064	* there exists a global section `s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` whose image under
1065	  the canonical inclusion `𝒪_C(P) ↪ 𝒦_C ≅ K(C)` equals `f` (formally,
1066	  `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField P hP s = f`);
1067	* the order conditions hold: `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor
1068	  `Q ∈ C.PrimeDivisor` whose generic point is not `P`, and
1069	  `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` (where the latter is read off the prime divisor
1070	  `⟨P, h⟩` with `h : Order.coheight P = 1` the codimension-one witness
1071	  automatic for a closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme).
1072	
1073	The iff is the substantive content of Hartshorne's Proposition 7.7(b) /
1074	its proof, specialised to `D_0 = [P]`.

1267	
1268	The two-dimensionality of `H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` and the one-dimensional
1269	constant subspace `k̄ · 1` give a non-zero quotient `H⁰/k̄`. Any lift

1271	`globalSections_iff`, a non-constant rational function `f ∈ K(C)` with
1272	the order conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`.

1274	`Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` from the basis `(1, f)`). -/
